epigraph.text: One thing I do know, that though I was blind, now I see.
epigraph.quote: The Central Limit Theorem is the most important theorem in probability theory.

content.text:
The Central Limit Theorem is perhaps the most important result in 
statistics. It explains why the normal distribution appears so 
pervasively and enables inference even when we don't know the 
population distribution.

content.title: Central Limit Theorem
content.content:
Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be a random sample from *any* 
distribution with mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$. Then 
as $n \to \infty$:
\[
 Z_n = \frac{\xbar - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} \Normal(0, 1)
\]
Equivalently, $\xbar$ is approximately $\Normal(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$ 
for large $n$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: The CLT Is About Sums and Averages
content.content:
The CLT applies to sums and means of random variables, not to 
individual observations. The population itself need not be normal.

content.text: The rate of convergence depends on the population shape:

items: **Slightly skewed:** $n \geq 30$ is a common rule of thumb

content.title: CLT in Action
content.problem:
The time between earthquakes in a region follows an 
exponential distribution with mean 100 days. For the next 40 earthquakes, 
find the probability the average waiting time is less than 90 days.
content.solution:
$\mu = 100$, $\sigma = 100$ (for exponential, $\sigma = \mu$), $n = 40$.

By CLT, $\xbar \approx \Normal(100, 100^2/40)$, so $\SE = 100/\sqrt{40} \approx 15.81$.

\[
 z = \frac{90 - 100}{15.81} \approx -0.63
\]

$P(\xbar < 90) = P(Z < -0.63) \approx 0.264$.

content.title: The Central Limit Theorem
content.content:
The CLT was first stated by Abraham de Moivre (1733) for binomial 
distributions, extended by Laplace, and proved in full generality 
by Lyapunov (1901). The name "Central Limit Theorem" was coined 
by George Pólya in 1920, reflecting its central importance to 
probability theory.

content.text: The CLT justifies using normal-based methods even when:
This is why the normal distribution appears throughout statistics.

The heavens declare the glory of God, 
and the sky above proclaims his handiwork. Day after day they pour 
forth speech.
